Fnrn Polrrrcal PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 e Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
November 2,2006
Lori J. Barker
Assistant City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
4ll Maan Street - 3rd Floor
P. O. Box 3420
Chico, CA 95927
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A.-06-188
Dear Ms. Barker:
This letter is inrespons;- to your request on behalf of David Kelley, planning
Commissioner for the City of Chico, for advice regarding the honorarium provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the *Act).r
QUESTION
Are payments to Commissioner Kelley for writing a periodic column for a local
newspaper considered honoraria and prohibited by the Act?
CONCLUSION
Based upon the facts submitted, we conclude that payments received by
Commissioner Kelley for writing a periodic column for aiocal newspaper are Lonoraria
and prohibited by the Act.
FACTS
Commissioner Kelley is a licensed and activelypracticing architect. The Chico
News & Review, a free weekly publication distributed throughoui the Chico urban area
containing articles primarily about local news and events, has offered to pay
Commissioner Kelley to write a periodic column. Commissioner Kelley-has indicated
that the topics of these columns would fall into three categories including items of local
interest, planning concepts, and architecture. Additionally, you note that although
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
::


File No. ,4.-06-188
Page No. 2
Commissioner Kelley is a licensed and active architect,the articles will not be written in
connection with a business that fits the definition of a "bona fide" business under
regulation 18932.1(a) or (b).
ANALYSIS
Section 89502(a) provides that an "individual specified in Section 87200 shall not
accept any honorarium." As a planning commissioner, Commissioner Kelley is an
individual specified in section 97200 andmay not accept anyhonorarium.
Section 89501(a) defines the term'honorarium," in pertinent part, as follows:
"(a) Forpurposes of this chapter, 'honorarium'means, except as
provided in subdivision (b), anypayment made in consideration foiany
speech grven, article published, or attendance at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.
(b) The term .honorarium, does not include:
(1) Earned income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or
profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insuran ce, real'
estate, banking, or building contracting, unless the sole or predominant
activity of the business, trade, or profession is making speiches. The
Commission shall adopt regulations to implement this subdivision.,,
Regulation 18937.2 also provides the foflowing in pertinentpart:
"(a) Forpurposes of Government code sections g9501 through
89506, an 'article published' means a nonfictional written work:
(1) That is produced in connection with any activity other than the
practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession; and
Q) That is published in a periodical, joumal, newspaper,
newsletter, magazine, pamphlet, or similar publication.,,
While this definition of the "article published" for purposes of the honorarium ban
at section 89502 is broad enough to include periodic columnsln a local newspapeE this
fact is not dispositive of your question. In fact, the periodic columns in a local newspaper
may fall under the exception in section S9501(b)(1) if,
"(1) The services are provided in connection with an individual,s
business or the individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide


File No. ,{-06-188
Page No. 3
business, trade, or profession, such as teaching practicing law, medicine,
insurance, real estate, banking, or building contracting, pirsuant to
[regulations 18932.1 through 1g932.3]; and
Q)Ttre services are customarilyprovided in connection with the
business, trade, or profession.,, (Regulation 1 g932(a).)
A trade or professional practitioner is presumed to be ..bona fide,, if ,.the
tradesperson or professional practitioner possesses current licensure or certification
which is issued by state or federal occupational licensing authority, the issuance of which
requires a demonstration of skill and knowledge in connection wiih ttre relevant trade or
profession." (Regulation I 8932.1 (cXl ).)
Based upon the facts provided, Commissioner Kelley is a licensed and practicing
architect. As a licensed architect, Commissioner Kelly is presumed to be a..bona fide,,
professionalpractitioner. The remaining question is whether periodic columns in a local
newspaper are "customarily provided" in connection with the business, trade or
profession.
The determl"{]9" of whetherperiodic columns in a local newspaper are
"customarilyprovided" in connection with Commissioner Kelley,s profession must be
made on a case-by-case basis. (Donald Advice Letter, No. 4-00-272; UelmanAdvice
Leffer, No. 4-99-281; and Greenwald Advice Leffer, No. A-95-290A.) over the yeaxs
we have provided the following advice pertaining to the determination of whether an
official's speech or other personal services were "customarily provided', in connection
with a business, trade, or profession:
o Proceeds received by a public official, who was also a medical professional and
had written over 300 articles and three textbooks, for publishing books/articles
was "earned income" and not considered honoraria if the booksTafticles were
published as part of the official's medical/professional activities. (DonaldAdvice
Letter, supra.)
o The earned income exception applies to lectures glven by a physician to health-
care professionals related to areas of medicine in which h" p.u"ti""s and does
research. (Phillips Advice Letter, No. ,{-91-1 37.) Incontrast, a speech regarding
healthcare to a group of companies that manufacture pharmaceuticals, diagnostic
equipment, and various medical supplies does not appear to be the type of service
that is customarilyprovided in connection with the practice ofmeditin e. (Filante
Advice Letter, A-92-141.)
o Payments received for conducting church activities and speaking as a licensed
minister at youth camps, couples retreats, weddings and funerals=are not
prohibited honoraria. (Boatwrtght Advice Letter, No. A-g3-323.) However, the


File No. ,{-06-188
Page No. 4
exception is limited to speaking engagements within the scope of the minister,s
duties and does not apply to speeches unconnected to that aitivity. (Metcalf
Advice Letter, No. 4-95-3 I 6.)
Cleatly,periodic columns in a local newspaper, related to items of local interest
and planning concepts, would be unconnected to Commissioner Kelley,s activities as a
professional architect. Accordingly, payments received for publishinjperiodic columns
related to items of local interest and planning concepts do not fall within the..earned
income" exception and would be prohibited honoraria under the Act,s provisions.
However, periodic columns related to architecture present a more difficult
analysis. Ultimately, you have not provided. any facts demonstrating that writing periodic
columns for a local newspaper would be a service "customaritypro,Iided,, in connection
with practicing architecture. While additional facts may estabiish that this service is
customary, absent a showing of these facts, it does not appear that receivirg fuy-*ts for
publishing periodic columns in a local newspaper, even if related to architelture, falls
within the "earned income" exception.2 AcCordingly, payments received by
Commissioner Kelley for publishing periodic columns retated to architecture would also
be prohibited honoraria under the Act,s provisions.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGL:meh
I:\Advicelks\06-188
2 our advice herein is limited to the facts provided. The commission does not actas a finder of
fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby (lg7 5) I FppC Ops. 7 l.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are payments to Commissioner Kelley for writing a periodic column for a local
newspaper considered honoraria and prohibited by the Act?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts submitted, we conclude that payments received by
Commissioner Kelley for writing a periodic column for aiocal newspaper are Lonoraria
and prohibited by the Act.
